chapters.title: Past Tense
chapters.description: Talk about completed past actions in Hindi.

lessons.prompt: What is the past tense of 'khana' (to eat) for 'main' (masculine)?
lessons.explanation: For transitive verbs in past, the subject takes 'ne': 'Maine khaaya' = I ate. The verb agrees with the object, not subject.

lessons.prompt: उसने किताब ___। (She read the book.)
lessons.explanation: 'Usne kitaab padhi' = She read the book. 'Ne' marks the agent. Verb 'padhi' agrees with 'kitaab' (feminine).

lessons.prompt: Translate: 'We went to Delhi yesterday'
lessons.hint: Intransitive verbs (like jaana) don't take 'ne'. 'Hum gaye' = we went. Verb agrees with subject.

lessons.prompt: When do you NOT use 'ne' in past tense?
lessons.explanation: Intransitive verbs (jaana, aana, hona, lagna) NEVER take 'ne'. 'Woh aayi' = she came (no ne).

lessons.prompt: मैंने उसे ___ (मदद करना — to help)। (I helped him.)
lessons.explanation: 'Maine uski madad ki' = I helped him. 'Madad karna' = to help. Verb 'ki' agrees with 'madad' (feminine).

chapters.title: Postpositions
chapters.description: Master Hindi postpositions — the equivalents of English prepositions.

lessons.prompt: What does 'के लिए (ke liye)' mean?
lessons.explanation: 'Ke liye' = for. 'Mere liye' = for me. 'Unke liye' = for them. Unlike English, postpositions come AFTER the noun.

lessons.prompt: वह दिल्ली ___ आया है। (He has come from Delhi.)
lessons.explanation: 'Se' = from/since/with. 'Delhi se' = from Delhi. Also used for comparisons: 'yeh usse bada hai' = this is bigger than that.

lessons.prompt: Translate: 'There is a book on the table'
lessons.hint: 'Par' (पर) = on/at. 'Mez par' = on the table. Postpositions follow the noun they relate to.

lessons.prompt: What case does a noun take before most postpositions?
lessons.explanation: Before postpositions, nouns change to oblique form. 'Ladka' (boy) → 'ladke ke saath' (with the boy). 'Ghar' → 'ghar mein' (in the house — no change for inanimate).

lessons.prompt: बच्चे स्कूल ___ हैं। (The children are at school.)
lessons.explanation: 'Mein' (में) = in/at (location). 'Skool mein' = at school. For specific location also: 'par'. 'Ghar mein' = at home.

chapters.title: Future Tense & Intentions
chapters.description: Express future plans, intentions, and predictions in Hindi.

lessons.prompt: What is the future tense of 'jaana' for 'main' (masculine)?
lessons.explanation: Future tense: verb stem + unga/ungi/unge. 'Jaunga' = I will go (masculine). Feminine: 'jaungi'.

lessons.prompt: वह कल भारत ___। (She will come to India tomorrow.)
lessons.explanation: 'Aayegi' = she will come (feminine future). 'Kal' = tomorrow. 'Bharat aayegi' = will come to India.

lessons.prompt: Translate: 'I am going to study Hindi' (near future intention)
lessons.hint: 'Wala hoon' construction = about to do / going to do. 'Padhne wala hoon' = about to study.

lessons.prompt: How do you express 'should' or 'ought to' in Hindi?
lessons.explanation: 'Chahiye' = should/need to (obligation/recommendation). 'Aapko yahaaN jaana chahiye' = You should go there.

lessons.prompt: हमें ज़्यादा पानी ___ चाहिए। (We should drink more water.)
lessons.explanation: 'Peena chahiye' = should drink. 'Hamein ... peena chahiye' = we should drink. Infinitive + chahiye for obligation.

chapters.title: Relative & Correlative Clauses
chapters.description: Use jo-woh and other relative structures in complex sentences.

lessons.prompt: What is the Hindi relative-correlative pair meaning 'whoever-that person'?
lessons.explanation: 'Jo...woh' = who/whoever...that (person). Hindi uses correlative pairs: jo/jab/jahan → woh/tab/wahan.

lessons.prompt: ___ आया, वह मेरा दोस्त था। (Whoever came, was my friend.)
lessons.explanation: 'Jo aaya, woh mera dost tha' = Whoever came, that person was my friend. Jo introduces the relative clause.

lessons.prompt: Translate: 'When he comes, I will tell him'
lessons.correctAnswer: जब वह आएगा, तब मैं उसे बताऊँगा
lessons.hint: 'Jab' = when, 'tab' = then. 'Aayega' = will come. 'Bataaunga' = I will tell.

lessons.prompt: What correlative pair means 'where...there'?
lessons.explanation: 'Jahan...wahan' = where...there. 'Jahan chaaho wahan jao' = Go wherever you want.

lessons.prompt: ___ खाओगे, उतना अच्छा लगेगा। (The more you eat, the better you'll feel.)
lessons.explanation: 'Jitna...utna' = the more...the more. 'Jitna padho, utna seekho' = The more you read, the more you learn.
